g.data-narration: Welcome to Binary Tree Traversals. Learn the essential algorithms for visiting tree nodes systematically.
g.text: BCS401 - Analysis and Design of Algorithms

g.data-narration: A binary tree is a hierarchical structure where each node has at most two children: left and right.
g.text: A hierarchical data structure where each node has
• Topmost node = Root | Nodes without children = Leaves

g.data-narration: Tree traversals enable searching, sorting, expression evaluation, and tree manipulation in computer science.
g.text: Tree traversals systematically visit all nodes - essential for:

g.data-narration: We will use this binary tree throughout our examples: root 10 with children 5 and 15.
g.text: Example Tree - We Will Traverse This
We will trace all four traversals on this tree:

g.data-narration: Inorder traversal visits nodes in order: Left subtree, then Node, then Right subtree. Acronym LNR.
g.text: Primary use: Binary Search Tree - produces sorted (ascending) output

g.data-narration: Inorder traversal of our example tree produces 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 20 - sorted ascending order.
g.text: For BST: This is ALWAYS sorted in ascending order!

g.data-narration: Preorder traversal visits: Node first, then Left subtree, then Right subtree. Acronym NLR.
g.text: Primary use: Tree copying, prefix notation, serialization

g.data-narration: Preorder traversal starts with root, visits all left children first: 10, 5, 3, 7, 15, 20.
g.text: Tracing: Visit 10 → Go left → Visit 5 → Visit 3 → 7 → Return → Visit 15 → 20
Starts with ROOT, ends with last leaf - great for copying tree structure!

g.data-narration: Postorder traversal visits: Left subtree, then Right subtree, then Node. Acronym LRN.
g.text: Primary use: Delete trees (free memory bottom-up), postfix evaluation

g.data-narration: Postorder traversal ends with root: 3, 7, 5, 20, 15, 10. Perfect for bottom-up tree deletion.
g.text: Tracing: Left subtree first → Right subtree → Finally ROOT at end
ROOT is always LAST - essential for memory deallocation!

g.data-narration: Level order traversal visits nodes level by level using a queue. It's breadth-first search (BFS).
g.text: Order: Level by Level, left to right using Queue
Primary use: Shortest path in unweighted graphs, level-by-level processing

g.data-narration: Level order visits by depth: Level 0: 10, Level 1: 5, 15, Level 2: 3, 7, 20.
g.text: Uses FIFO Queue: Dequeue current, Enqueue children

g.data-narration: Expression trees apply traversals to evaluate expressions: Inorder gives infix, Preorder prefix, Postorder postfix.
g.text: Different traversals produce different notation forms:

g.data-narration: All traversals have O(n) time complexity. Space complexity is O(h) for recursive, O(n) for level order.
g.text: Time is always O(n) - each node visited exactly once

g.data-narration: Remember: Inorder for sorted BST, Preorder for copying, Postorder for deletion, Level order for BFS.
Exam tips: Draw tree first, remember acronyms LNR NLR LRN, verify by counting nodes equals n.
g.text: 1. Remember: L=Left, N=Node, R=Right | 2. For BST: Inorder = sorted | 3. Preorder: root FIRST
4. Postorder: root LAST (for deletion) | 5. Level Order uses QUEUE | 6. Time always O(n)
7. Space: O(h) for recursive, O(n) for queue | 8. Draw tree first, then trace | 9. Count = n nodes
